item.title: Small error
item.content:encoded:
“He was picked up by Facility guards who had been dispatched to the station following GPS locating the D-classes device.“

I believe it should be D-class’s

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really, it's a good article, but all of the above combined also left me to no vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have to agree on RockTeethMothEyes with this one, something about this article rubs me the wrong way and I can't quite point out as to why.

Maybe it's the concept of people suddenly being taken to places most that are most meaningful to them; maybe it's the idea that this skip is almost similar to 348 (in the way that it reminds subjects of people they love, except this takes them to places that's related to people they love) but doesn't have the impact the latter has nor do I feel sympathy to those that returned home.

I do have to wonder as to why an MTF recruit seemed to recall the memories of others inside the train but there's no report or interview from Researcher Adell, what they've seen, or what they've done inside.

I also question the purpose of the Victorian-era Spanish lady and would've asked: "why was she relevant" if not re-reading the logs and noticed that the film she holds onto is the first "dream" that we see and said film reel is recovered at the final expedition. While both stories make each other relevant, I question the purpose in the grand scheme of things and how it develops the story further.

While the tone fits with the wiki's standards (as far as I know) and the idea of a train delivering people back to their doorsteps is interesting, not much is really expanded on this idea.

No vote for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm less than impressed. I find that some of the tone is a bit off in places to favor a more prose-like writing style, specifically in the last three logs (an easy fix if styled as if they are video logs instead of brief snippets of summaries). Additionally, I take some issues with the grammar here, particularly in places where there are multiple clauses and either commas are used too frequently or are completely missing. It's a bit of a tough read, as I found myself often revisiting sentences, and that some bits of information never come back into play (specifically the "neon red heart" lines)

However, I mostly don't really care for how the concept is executed. It doesn't really go anywhere with the premise, and if it does, I don't understand where it is has gone. It truly feels like it's repeating information that we understand (in the case of the testing log) and that it is conveying new information that doesn't add anything to the story told (in the case of the final logs).

Given that the above comments have expressed a satisfactory read, I am left to conclude that I have to be missing something here, because I found this to be incredibly lacking.

item.title: My review #2
item.content:encoded:
Great idea! Bit small but I think it's quite satisfactory.

8/10 in general

Great premise, great execution in general.

item.title: My Review
item.content:encoded: This SCP article checks all the boxes. Good clinical tone? Yep. Interesting premise? Definitely. Great execution? 100%. This is a great SCP, no doubt about it. A definite +1 from me.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Home is where the heart is.

Thanks to all my critters, this has undergone a number of revisions and crit sessions and I hope to have found a balance.

Big thanks to GerrymanderBassist, PsychLantern and Scheals for initial crit. Thanks to JakdragonX for trimming and refinement. Thanks to DrDromeus and Zoobeeny for helping restructure the ending and overall crit. Thanks to OliviaMonkey, BlueJones and cybersqyd for final draft crit. Last but certainly not least thanks to SharpEmbrace who helped with the initial idea, grammar checks, story crit and after posting crit… Just about everything really.

Hope you enjoy!

Quick side note. The image was taken by me, hence the lack of a source. :D

Edit: Added a BHL themed ACS with less garish colours to see how it does.
